what_this_pass_proved: Players can place a point on the unit circle by clicking, using the four quadrant labels (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) as visual anchors — the basic spatial mapping is learnable.
The three-representation system (degrees → radians → coordinates) can be introduced sequentially within a single 15-round session without losing the player's understanding of the core task.
Tiered tolerance (15° → 10° → 8° → 5°) creates a legible difficulty ramp without changing the interaction model — players perceive increasing precision demand as the session progresses.
The retry-on-close mechanic is implementable and pedagogically honest: showing the ghost position during the second attempt turns a near-miss into a guided placement rather than a failed round.
open_questions: Do players mentally connect the coordinate display (cos θ, sin θ) to their topping position on the circle during play, or do they treat it as decorative readout?
For radian prompts (π/6, π/3, 5π/6, etc.), do players who have not memorized the conversions use any estimation strategy, or do they guess? This determines whether Pass 2 needs a conversion scaffold.
Is 15 rounds the right session length? A player who misses the first few may disengage before reaching Tier 2. Would 8–10 rounds prove the same things with a higher completion rate?
Does the ghost position revealed after a close/miss teach the correct location, or give away the answer in a way that removes the spatial reasoning work?
Does the pizza/topping metaphor add meaningful context, or does it dilute the mathematical framing for older learners?

bugs_fixed.description: 'Close! Try again.' was displayed after near-miss results but the game auto-advanced after 1800ms with no retry mechanism. The text promised an action the player could not take. Fixed: feedback text changed to 'Close — adjust your aim'; a real one-retry system implemented.

Retry implementation: retryState machine (null → 'offered' → 'active') added. First close result pauses the round and shows 'Try Again ↺'. Ghost position remains visible during offered and active states as a placement guide. Second attempt always auto-advances regardless of result.

known_limits: Click-to-place only — no touch drag or continuous sweep. Motor precision is limited by click granularity.

Ghost position revealed after close/miss may reduce spatial reasoning effort on the retry. Trade-off is intentional: teaching value of guided placement was judged higher than preserving full self-discovery.
Session length (15 rounds) was not validated against attention span for the target age range.
Score is displayed but has no mechanical consequence — no progression unlock, no replay incentive tied to score.
Only standard angles are tested (multiples of 30° and 45°). Non-standard angle placement is not part of this prototype.
The coordinate display shows cos/sin live during placement but there is no mechanism checking whether the player is reading it to calibrate.

auditor_results.notes: Auditor not formally run against Unit Circle parameters — tolerance values (15°/10°/8°/5°) follow a consistent decreasing pattern but no ParameterConfig has been defined for this game type. Ramp shape is smooth by inspection. No UNIT_CIRCLE_PARAMETER_CONFIGS exist yet.

next_pass_first_obligation: Determine whether players scoring 'close' on radian prompts (π/6, π/3, etc.) are estimating from the degree equivalent or guessing — this evidence decides whether Pass 2 should add a conversion scaffold (e.g. a fraction-of-circle visual) or maintain the current direct-placement model.
